title: Acute BRONCHIOLITIS (RSV-Positive) in a 5-Month-Old Former 35-Week Late-Preterm Infant Girl Presenting in Mid-Winter with 3 Days of URI Progressing to TACHYPNEA + RETRACTIONS + Crackles/Wheezing + DECREASED ORAL FEEDING (Risk Factors for Severe Disease: Young Infant, Late Preterm, Winter, Household Smoke Exposure): Mostly SUPPORTIVE Nursing Care ('LESS IS MORE') — Elevate the Head of Bed, NASAL SALINE + BULB SUCTION BEFORE FEEDS and PRN (the Most Evidence-Based Intervention), Small Frequent Feeds with NG/IV Hydration Only If Intake Inadequate, Oxygen for Persistent SpO2 < 90-92%, MINIMAL HANDLING with Cluster Care, CONTACT + DROPLET Isolation with Cohorting + Strict Hand Hygiene + Gown/Gloves/Mask, Watchful Monitoring for APNEA (Especially Young Infants), Increased Work of Breathing, and Dehydration — and Explicitly NO Routine BRONCHODILATORS, NO Routine SYSTEMIC CORTICOSTEROIDS, NO Routine ANTIBIOTICS, NO Routine Chest X-Ray, NO Deep Suctioning: Family Teaching and Discharge Criteria, and PREVENTION (Hand Hygiene, Smoke-Free Home, PALIVIZUMAB/NIRSEVIMAB for High-Risk Infants, Maternal RSV Vaccination in Pregnancy)
subtitle: BRONCHIOLITIS is the most common lower-respiratory tract infection in infants and one of the most over-treated conditions in pediatrics — making it a paradigm NCLEX 'less is more' case. The illness is INFLAMMATION and OBSTRUCTION of the small bronchioles from a VIRAL infection (most often RESPIRATORY SYNCYTIAL VIRUS — RSV — and also rhinovirus, human metapneumovirus, parainfluenza) leading to upper-airway congestion followed in 1-3 days by lower-respiratory involvement (TACHYPNEA, WHEEZING, CRACKLES, RETRACTIONS, NASAL FLARING, grunting), DECREASED ORAL FEEDING, and — in the youngest infants (especially under 2-3 months) — APNEA. The illness peaks in late fall through early spring; almost all children are infected by age 2. RISK FACTORS for SEVERE bronchiolitis: AGE < 12 weeks (especially < 6 weeks — apnea risk), PREMATURITY (any, but particularly < 32 weeks), chronic lung disease of prematurity (BPD), congenital heart disease (especially hemodynamically significant), immunodeficiency, neuromuscular disease, and exposure to TOBACCO SMOKE. DIAGNOSIS is CLINICAL; routine chest X-ray and routine viral testing are NOT recommended (rapid RSV testing is used selectively to guide isolation/cohorting and to inform palivizumab decisions when relevant — not to direct routine care); routine laboratory studies are NOT recommended. NURSING CARE is MOSTLY SUPPORTIVE — the AAP guideline frames this clearly: do what helps and stop doing what does not. PRIMARY interventions: (1) NASAL SUCTIONING with saline drops + bulb suction (or a NoseFrida-style aspirator) BEFORE FEEDS, BEFORE SLEEP, and as needed — this is the most evidence-based single intervention and improves feeding and comfort; (2) POSITIONING — elevate the head of bed; comfort positioning held by a parent when possible; (3) HYDRATION — small frequent oral feeds; if intake is inadequate, use nasogastric or IV fluids (no absolute respiratory-rate cutoff — base on work of breathing + safe swallow + dehydration); (4) OXYGEN if SpO2 is persistently below approximately 90-92% (institutional variation; avoid over-oxygenation and continuous monitoring of well-appearing infants for transient dips); (5) MINIMAL HANDLING and CLUSTER CARE (agitation worsens respiratory distress); (6) ISOLATION with CONTACT + DROPLET precautions (gown, gloves, mask, dedicated equipment) and COHORTING of RSV-positive patients; STRICT HAND HYGIENE is the single most important infection-prevention step; (7) WATCHFUL MONITORING for APNEA (especially in young infants), increasing work of breathing, falling SpO2 or rising O2 requirement, poor feeding, dehydration, and lethargy; and (8) escalation as needed — heated high-flow nasal cannula (HHFNC) → CPAP → endotracheal intubation. SECONDARY/'do not do' (high-yield NCLEX): do NOT use routine BRONCHODILATORS (albuterol/levalbuterol) — they do not improve outcomes (a trial is occasionally considered in a child with atopy or family history of asthma but is stopped if no benefit); do NOT use routine SYSTEMIC CORTICOSTEROIDS; do NOT use routine ANTIBIOTICS (viral); do NOT use routine RACEMIC EPINEPHRINE in inpatients; do NOT obtain routine chest X-ray; do NOT use DEEP SUCTIONING (nasal/oral pharynx only — deep beyond the nasopharynx has no evidence and may worsen); do NOT use ribavirin routinely. ESCALATION TRIGGERS to alert the team: progressive retractions, grunting, nasal flaring; apnea; new or rising O2 requirement; falling SpO2 despite suctioning and positioning; poor feeding/dehydration; lethargy or altered mental status. DISCHARGE CRITERIA: stable SpO2 in room air, adequate oral feeding, hydration, family able to monitor and provide care at home, and reliable follow-up. FAMILY TEACHING: nasal saline + bulb suction technique (BEFORE FEEDS + BEFORE SLEEP + PRN); small frequent feeds with signs of dehydration to report (fewer wet diapers, sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, lethargy); signs of worsening (faster or harder breathing, retractions, decreased feeding, lethargy, cyanosis); strict hand hygiene; SMOKE-FREE home/car (a key modifiable factor); usual course 7-14 days with cough that can persist; when to seek care; safe-sleep reinforcement. PREVENTION: hand hygiene; avoid sick contacts; smoke-free environment; PALIVIZUMAB (Synagis) monthly during RSV season for selected high-risk infants (per AAP criteria — e.g., premature with chronic lung disease, hemodynamically significant CHD); NIRSEVIMAB (Beyfortus), a long-acting monoclonal antibody, is recommended for all infants entering or during their first RSV season (per current immunization guidance), and MATERNAL RSV VACCINATION during pregnancy provides infant protection. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: giving routine albuterol, systemic steroids, antibiotics, or racemic epinephrine; deep suctioning; obtaining unnecessary chest X-rays; over-monitoring well-appearing infants with continuous pulse oximetry; not suctioning the nose before feeds; under-recognizing apnea in the youngest infants; missing dehydration; missing the smoke-exposure history; and breaching contact + droplet precautions. NURSING ROLE: assess severity and risk; deliver supportive care anchored in nasal saline + bulb suction, small frequent feeds (with NG/IV if needed), positioning, and minimal handling; oxygenate per the threshold; maintain contact + droplet precautions and strict hand hygiene; watch closely for apnea, worsening work of breathing, and dehydration; escalate to HHFNC/CPAP/intubation when needed; teach the family (suctioning technique, signs to report, smoke-free home); and apply prevention strategies including palivizumab/nirsevimab and maternal RSV vaccination as appropriate.

patient.name: Baby Girl M. Okafor
patient.code: Full code; pediatric inpatient unit; goal = supportive nursing care anchored in nasal saline + bulb suction, small frequent feeds (NG/IV if intake inadequate), positioning, minimal handling/cluster care, oxygen for persistent SpO2 < 90-92%, contact + droplet isolation with strict hand hygiene, watchful monitoring for apnea/worsening work of breathing/dehydration, escalation to HHFNC/CPAP/intubation if needed, family teaching, and prevention (smoke-free home; palivizumab/nirsevimab/maternal RSV vaccine as appropriate)
patient.admitDate: Mid-winter presentation. Former 35-week late-preterm infant (now 5 months old) brought to the ED with 3 days of upper-respiratory symptoms (rhinorrhea, congestion, mild cough) progressing over the past day to TACHYPNEA, WHEEZING and CRACKLES, RETRACTIONS, nasal flaring, decreased oral feeding (about 50% of normal), and one episode of brief apnea reported by mother during a feed. Rapid RSV antigen POSITIVE. SpO2 89% on room air → 95% with low-flow nasal cannula 0.5 L. Adequate urine output but mother reports fewer wet diapers. Father smokes outside but the smoking jacket is brought into the home (residual smoke exposure). Older brother (age 4) has a 'cold.' Admitted for supportive care and monitoring. Family is anxious.
patient.room: Pediatric inpatient unit; private room with contact + droplet isolation; cohorting with other RSV+ patients per facility; cardiopulmonary + pulse-oximetry monitoring (continuous given young age + reported apnea); family presence supported

vitals.spo2: 89% on room air → 95% on 0.5 L NC
vitals.temp: 37.9°C; bilateral crackles + wheezing; mild nasal flaring; decreased oral intake (~50% of usual); one brief apneic episode reported during feed at home; ill but consolable in mother's arms; mother visibly anxious

37.8°C; nasal saline drops + bulb suction performed before next feed (the most evidence-based intervention) — moderate clear secretions cleared; HOB elevated; infant held upright by mother (comfort positioning) → respiratory effort improved; cluster care implemented (minimize agitation)

37.7°C; supportive care anchored — no routine albuterol, no systemic corticosteroids, no routine antibiotics (viral), no racemic epinephrine, no routine chest X-ray, no deep suctioning beyond nasopharynx; small frequent feeds offered after nasal suction; CONTACT + DROPLET precautions in place (gown, gloves, mask, dedicated equipment, cohorting, strict hand hygiene)

37.6°C; oral intake remains ~60% of usual → NG feeds initiated for the next 24-48h to ensure hydration without aspiration risk (low-rate continuous or scheduled bolus per protocol); continuous CR monitor + pulse ox given age + reported apnea; escalation pathway prepared (heated high-flow nasal cannula → CPAP → intubation) if work of breathing worsens

37.2°C; weaned off O2; feeding ~75% of usual; nasal suctioning before feeds continued; no further apnea; respiratory effort improved; mother able to demonstrate (teach-back) bulb-suction technique, signs to report, hand-hygiene, and smoke-free home plan; brother kept distanced during illness

36.9°C; meets discharge criteria — stable SpO2 in room air, adequate feeding/hydration, family able to monitor at home, reliable follow-up; family teach-back verified; smoke-free home plan reinforced; nirsevimab (or palivizumab eligibility per protocol) discussed; maternal RSV vaccination at the family's next pregnancy mentioned; follow-up scheduled

labs.v: 5-month-old, former 35-week late-preterm infant, presenting in mid-winter with 3 days of upper-respiratory symptoms progressing to tachypnea, retractions, crackles/wheezing, decreased oral feeding (~50% of usual), and a brief reported apneic episode during a feed. SpO2 89% on room air → 95% on low-flow nasal cannula. Rapid RSV antigen POSITIVE. The diagnosis is CLINICAL; viral testing is used selectively for isolation/cohorting (and to inform palivizumab) — not to direct routine care. RISK FACTORS for severe disease present: AGE < 12 weeks is the highest-risk window (this infant is 5 months — slightly out of the peak-apnea window but still at risk given prematurity + history of an apneic episode), LATE-PRETERM birth, winter season, and household tobacco-smoke exposure. PMH: routine immunizations to date; no chronic lung disease/BPD; no congenital heart disease. SOCIAL: father smokes outside (residual smoke exposure remains a factor); older brother (4 y) with a 'cold' (likely source of exposure). The nursing task: supportive care + protective monitoring + family teaching + prevention.
labs.r: Bronchiolitis = viral inflammation/obstruction of small bronchioles (RSV most common; also rhinovirus/hMPV/parainfluenza). Peak severity 2-6 mo. CLINICAL diagnosis — routine CXR + routine labs + routine viral testing NOT recommended (rapid RSV used selectively for isolation/cohorting + palivizumab decisions). Risk for severe disease: age < 12 wk (especially < 6 wk apnea risk), preterm, CLD/BPD, hemodynamically significant CHD, immunodeficiency, neuromuscular disease, smoke exposure. RN role: assess severity + risk; deliver supportive care.

labs.n: PRIMARY nursing interventions (the 'do' list — supportive, mostly comfort/airway/feeding/safety)
labs.v: The AAP guideline frames care simply: do what helps and stop doing what does not. PRIMARY interventions: (1) NASAL SUCTIONING with saline drops + bulb suction (or NoseFrida-style aspirator) BEFORE FEEDS, BEFORE SLEEP, and as needed — this is the most evidence-based single intervention and improves feeding and comfort; deep suctioning beyond the nasopharynx is NOT recommended (no benefit, may worsen). (2) POSITIONING — elevate the head of bed; comfort positioning held by a parent when possible; calming environment to reduce agitation. (3) HYDRATION — offer small frequent oral feeds; if intake is inadequate or the work of breathing is unsafe for oral feeding, use NASOGASTRIC or IV fluids (there is no absolute respiratory-rate cutoff — base the decision on work of breathing, safe swallow, dehydration). (4) OXYGEN if SpO2 is persistently below approximately 90-92% (institutional variation; avoid over-oxygenation; do NOT pursue brief asymptomatic dips that resolve on their own). (5) MINIMAL HANDLING + CLUSTER CARE (agitation worsens respiratory distress). (6) ISOLATION with CONTACT + DROPLET precautions — gown, gloves, mask, dedicated equipment — and COHORTING of RSV-positive patients; STRICT HAND HYGIENE is the single most important infection-prevention step. (7) WATCHFUL MONITORING for APNEA (especially in young or preterm infants), increasing work of breathing, falling SpO2 or rising O2 requirement, poor feeding, dehydration, and lethargy. (8) Escalation as needed — heated high-flow nasal cannula (HHFNC) → CPAP → endotracheal intubation.
labs.r: DO: nasal saline + BULB SUCTION (most evidence-based) BEFORE FEEDS + BEFORE SLEEP + PRN (NO deep suction beyond nasopharynx); ELEVATE HOB + parent comfort positioning + calm environment; SMALL FREQUENT FEEDS, NG/IV if inadequate (no RR cutoff — assess WOB/swallow/hydration); OXYGEN if persistent SpO2 < ~90-92% (avoid over-O2); MINIMAL HANDLING + cluster care; CONTACT + DROPLET isolation + COHORT + strict hand hygiene + PPE; WATCH for apnea (young/preterm) + ↑WOB + ↓SpO2 + poor feeding + dehydration; ESCALATE HHFNC → CPAP → intubation. RN role: supportive care framework.

labs.n: SECONDARY — the 'do NOT do' list (high-yield NCLEX) + escalation + family teaching
labs.v: Do NOT use routine BRONCHODILATORS (albuterol/levalbuterol) — they do NOT improve outcomes; a single trial may be considered in a child with atopy or family history of asthma but is DISCONTINUED if no benefit. Do NOT use routine SYSTEMIC CORTICOSTEROIDS. Do NOT use routine ANTIBIOTICS — bronchiolitis is VIRAL; reserve for clear bacterial superinfection. Do NOT use routine RACEMIC EPINEPHRINE in inpatients. Do NOT obtain a routine chest X-ray (only if a different diagnosis is suspected). Do NOT use DEEP suctioning beyond the nasopharynx (no evidence; may worsen). Do NOT use ribavirin routinely. ESCALATION TRIGGERS to alert the team: progressive retractions, grunting, nasal flaring; APNEA; new or rising O2 requirement; falling SpO2 despite suctioning and positioning; poor feeding/dehydration; lethargy or altered mental status. Pathway: HHFNC → CPAP → intubation. FAMILY TEACHING is central: nasal saline + bulb suction technique (BEFORE FEEDS + BEFORE SLEEP + PRN); small frequent feeds with signs of dehydration to report (fewer wet diapers, sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, lethargy); signs of WORSENING (faster or harder breathing, deeper retractions, decreased feeding, lethargy, color change/cyanosis); strict hand hygiene at home; SMOKE-FREE home and car (residual smoke from a 'smoking jacket' still exposes the infant — counsel on cessation resources); usual course 7-14 days with cough that can persist; when to seek care; safe-sleep reinforcement.
labs.r: DO NOT routinely: ALBUTEROL (no benefit), systemic STEROIDS, ANTIBIOTICS, racemic EPI inpatient, CXR, DEEP suctioning, ribavirin. ESCALATION triggers: progressive retractions/grunting/flaring, APNEA, new/rising O2 need, falling SpO2 despite suction/position, poor feeding/dehydration, lethargy → HHFNC → CPAP → intubation. FAMILY TEACHING: bulb-suction technique BEFORE FEEDS + sleep, small frequent feeds, signs of dehydration + worsening, hand hygiene, SMOKE-FREE home/car (residual smoke from 'smoking jacket' still exposes), course 7-14 d, when to seek care, safe sleep.

labs.n: Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors + PREVENTION (smoke-free + palivizumab/nirsevimab + maternal RSV vaccine)
labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT give routine albuterol/levalbuterol, systemic corticosteroids, antibiotics, or racemic epinephrine; did NOT obtain a routine chest X-ray; did NOT use deep suctioning beyond the nasopharynx; did NOT continuously pulse-oximeter monitor a well-appearing older infant for transient dips (institutional variation — but use clinically); did NOT skip nasal suctioning before feeds; did NOT under-recognize apnea risk in a young/preterm infant; did NOT miss the smoke-exposure history; did NOT breach contact + droplet precautions; and did NOT discharge before stable feeding, oxygenation, and family teach-back. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: timely supportive care anchored in nasal saline + bulb suction; correct positioning + cluster care + minimal handling; appropriate oxygen threshold; NG/IV hydration when oral intake was inadequate; contact + droplet isolation with strict hand hygiene and cohorting; vigilant apnea + work-of-breathing + dehydration monitoring; escalation pathway readiness (HHFNC → CPAP → intubation); robust family teaching (suctioning + feeds + warning signs + smoke-free home); prevention reinforcement. PREVENTION: hand hygiene; avoid sick contacts; smoke-free home/car (counsel cessation; addresses residual smoke from a 'smoking jacket'); PALIVIZUMAB (Synagis) monthly during RSV season for selected high-risk infants per AAP criteria (e.g., premature with chronic lung disease, hemodynamically significant CHD); NIRSEVIMAB (Beyfortus) — long-acting RSV monoclonal antibody — for all infants entering or during their first RSV season per current immunization guidance; MATERNAL RSV vaccine during pregnancy provides infant protection through transferred antibodies. EXPECTED outcome: gradual respiratory and feeding recovery over days; safe discharge with strong family preparation and a clear smoke-free + prevention plan.
labs.r: Negatives: no routine albuterol/steroids/abx/racemic epi/CXR/deep suction; no continuous-monitoring of well infants; no skipping nasal suction before feeds; no under-recognition of apnea in young/preterm; no missed smoke history; no precautions breach; no premature discharge. Protective: nasal saline + bulb suction + positioning + cluster care + appropriate O2 + NG/IV hydration prn + contact+droplet+cohort+hand hygiene + apnea/WOB/dehydration monitoring + escalation pathway + family teaching + smoke-free + prevention. PREVENTION: hand hygiene + avoid sick contacts + SMOKE-FREE + PALIVIZUMAB (per AAP) + NIRSEVIMAB (all infants entering/during 1st RSV season) + MATERNAL RSV VACCINE in pregnancy. RN role: supportive care + protection + prevention.

labs.v: Positive (used for isolation/cohorting and palivizumab eligibility; does NOT direct routine care)

labs.n: SpO2 at admission on room air
labs.v: 89% (below threshold; supplemental low-flow O2 initiated to maintain ≥ 90-92%; wean off as tolerated)

Capillary refill < 2 sec; mucous membranes moist; anterior fontanelle level — adequately hydrated at admission with mother reporting fewer wet diapers (~ 4-5/day) — supports adding NG feeds if oral intake remains ~ 50-60% of usual
labs.r: Cap refill < 2 sec; mucous membranes moist; flat fontanelle; ≥ 6 wet diapers/day at this age

labs.v: Not performed (not routinely indicated in bronchiolitis; would be obtained only if an alternative diagnosis were suspected — e.g., pneumonia, foreign body, congenital cardiac anatomy)

nursesNote: Peds inpatient — 5-mo-old former 35-wk late-preterm infant, mid-winter, 3 d URI → progressive tachypnea + RETRACTIONS + crackles/wheezing + decreased oral feeding (~50%) + brief apneic episode at home reported by mom; rapid RSV+. Admit SpO2 89% RA → 95% on 0.5 L NC. Brother (4y) w/ 'cold'; father smokes outside but residual smoke from 'smoking jacket' = ongoing exposure. PROTECTIVE precautions: CONTACT + DROPLET isolation; private room w/ RSV+ cohorting; gown + gloves + mask + dedicated equipment; STRICT hand hygiene (#1 IC step). PRIMARY supportive care: NASAL SALINE + BULB SUCTION BEFORE FEEDS + before sleep + PRN (most evidence-based intervention) — moderate clear secretions cleared; HOB elevated + comfort positioning in mother's arms; CLUSTER CARE/minimal handling; SMALL FREQUENT feeds; oxygen 0.5 L NC to maintain SpO2 ≥ 90-92% (avoid over-O2); continuous CR monitor + pulse ox given age + reported apnea. Oral intake stayed ~60% → NG feeds started for next 24-48 h to ensure hydration w/o aspiration (no absolute RR cutoff — based on WOB/swallow/dehydration assessment). 'DO NOT' (high-yield): NO routine albuterol/levalbuterol, NO systemic corticosteroids, NO antibiotics (viral), NO racemic epi inpatient, NO routine CXR, NO deep suctioning beyond nasopharynx, NO ribavirin. ESCALATION pathway prepared (progressive retractions/grunting/flaring, apnea, rising O2 need, falling SpO2 despite suction/position, poor feeding/dehydration, lethargy → HHFNC → CPAP → intubation). FAMILY TEACHING (teach-back verified by mom): bulb-suction technique BEFORE feeds + sleep; small frequent feeds w/ dehydration signs (fewer wet diapers, sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, lethargy); worsening signs (faster/harder breathing, deeper retractions, decreased feeding, lethargy, color change → seek care); strict hand hygiene; SMOKE-FREE home/car w/ cessation resources (jacket residual smoke counts); usual course 7-14 d w/ cough lingering; safe sleep. Day 2: weaned off O2; feeding ~75%; no further apnea; respiratory effort improved. DISCHARGE: stable RA SpO2 + adequate feeding/hydration + family ready + reliable follow-up. PREVENTION: smoke-free reinforced; NIRSEVIMAB for all infants entering/during 1st RSV season per current immunization guidance discussed; PALIVIZUMAB eligibility per AAP/protocol; MATERNAL RSV vaccine during next pregnancy mentioned. NEGATIVES (correct practice): didn't give routine albuterol/steroids/abx/racemic epi/CXR/deep suction; didn't skip nasal suction before feeds; didn't under-recognize apnea risk in young/preterm; didn't miss smoke history; didn't breach contact + droplet precautions; didn't discharge before stable feeding + O2 + family teach-back.
providerOrders: ADMIT: pediatric inpatient unit for supportive care for RSV-positive acute bronchiolitis in a 5-month-old former 35-week late-preterm infant with one reported brief apneic episode at home

ISOLATION: contact + droplet precautions; private room (or cohort with other RSV-positive patients); gown, gloves, mask; dedicated equipment; strict hand hygiene as the single most important infection-prevention step
MONITORING: continuous cardiopulmonary + pulse oximetry given age and reported apnea; vital signs and work-of-breathing assessment per protocol; intake/output and daily weight

RESPIRATORY/AIRWAY: nasal saline drops + bulb suction (or NoseFrida-style aspirator) before feeds, before sleep, and as needed; do NOT use deep suctioning beyond the nasopharynx; elevate head of bed; comfort positioning held by parent; minimize handling/cluster care
OXYGEN: supplemental low-flow nasal cannula if SpO2 is persistently below approximately 90-92%; wean as tolerated; avoid over-oxygenation

HYDRATION/FEEDING: small frequent oral feeds as tolerated; nasogastric or IV fluids if oral intake is inadequate or work of breathing precludes safe feeding (do not require an absolute respiratory-rate cutoff — base on work of breathing, swallow safety, hydration)

DO NOT (routine): bronchodilators (albuterol/levalbuterol), systemic corticosteroids, antibiotics, racemic epinephrine, chest X-ray, deep suctioning, ribavirin — reserve antibiotics for clear bacterial superinfection

ESCALATION: heated high-flow nasal cannula → CPAP → endotracheal intubation for progressive retractions/grunting/flaring, apnea, new/rising O2 requirement, falling SpO2 despite suction/position, poor feeding/dehydration, lethargy

FAMILY TEACHING: nasal saline + bulb suction technique (before feeds and before sleep); small frequent feeds and signs of dehydration to report; warning signs of worsening (faster/harder breathing, retractions, decreased feeding, lethargy, cyanosis); strict hand hygiene; smoke-free home and car with cessation resources

PREVENTION: nirsevimab (Beyfortus) for all infants entering or during their first RSV season per current immunization guidance; palivizumab (Synagis) for selected high-risk infants per AAP/insurance protocol; maternal RSV vaccination during a future pregnancy to provide infant protection
DISCHARGE CRITERIA: stable SpO2 in room air, adequate oral feeding and hydration, family able to monitor and provide care at home with teach-back verification, reliable follow-up scheduled

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters about this RSV bronchiolitis admission?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Classic clinical bronchiolitis picture (URI → tachypnea + retractions + crackles/wheezing + decreased feeding) with rapid RSV positive in a former late-preterm 5-month-old infant — supportive care is the framework
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the diagnosis is clinical and care is supportive. RN role: recognize + frame supportive care.

opts.text: Risk factors for severe disease present (former late-preterm; brief apneic episode reported at home; household smoke exposure even though father smokes outside; winter season) warrant close monitoring and inpatient care
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — risk-factor cluster shapes intensity of monitoring. RN role: assess risk.

opts.text: PRIMARY supportive interventions: NASAL SALINE + BULB SUCTION before feeds and before sleep (the most evidence-based intervention), HOB elevation + comfort positioning, MINIMAL HANDLING/cluster care
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these are the high-yield supportive interventions. RN role: deliver supportive care.

opts.text: HYDRATION via small frequent feeds with NG or IV if intake is inadequate; OXYGEN if SpO2 persistently below ~ 90-92%; CONTACT + DROPLET isolation with strict hand hygiene and cohorting
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — hydration, oxygenation, and infection control complete the supportive framework. RN role: feeds + O2 + isolation.

opts.text: Do NOT routinely give bronchodilators (albuterol), systemic corticosteroids, antibiotics, racemic epinephrine, or obtain a routine chest X-ray; do NOT use deep suctioning beyond the nasopharynx
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the high-yield 'less is more' list. RN role: avoid non-beneficial interventions.

opts.text: Watch for APNEA (especially in young/preterm infants), increasing work of breathing, falling SpO2 or rising O2 need, poor feeding/dehydration, lethargy — and escalate to HHFNC → CPAP → intubation as needed; teach the family and reinforce prevention (smoke-free + nirsevimab/palivizumab + maternal RSV vaccine)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — escalation triggers and teaching/prevention close the loop. RN role: monitor + escalate + teach + prevention.

opts.text: Start a routine course of nebulized albuterol and IV ceftriaxone for the wheezing infant
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Routine bronchodilators are not recommended in bronchiolitis (no outcome benefit); antibiotics are reserved for clear bacterial superinfection.

opts.text: Perform deep nasotracheal suctioning every 2 hours regardless of need to 'clear secretions' and skip nasal saline before feeds
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Deep suctioning beyond the nasopharynx has no evidence and may worsen; nasal saline + bulb suction before feeds is the most evidence-based intervention.

opts.text: Bronchiolitis is a viral lower-respiratory infection — most often RSV — diagnosed CLINICALLY; routine viral testing, routine CXR, and routine labs are NOT recommended
opts.rat: CORRECT — the diagnostic principle. Foundation for avoiding overtesting.

opts.text: Highest-risk infants for severe disease include those < 12 weeks (especially < 6 weeks for apnea), preterm, with chronic lung disease/BPD, hemodynamically significant CHD, immunodeficiency, neuromuscular disease, and tobacco-smoke exposure
opts.rat: CORRECT — the risk-factor principle. Foundation for stratifying monitoring.

opts.text: The PRIMARY supportive interventions are nasal saline + bulb suction before feeds and sleep, HOB elevation/comfort positioning, small frequent feeds with NG/IV if inadequate, oxygen for persistent SpO2 < ~ 90-92%, minimal handling, and contact + droplet isolation with hand hygiene
opts.rat: CORRECT — the supportive-care principle. Foundation for safe nursing care.

opts.text: Routine BRONCHODILATORS, systemic CORTICOSTEROIDS, ANTIBIOTICS, RACEMIC EPINEPHRINE, routine CXR, and deep suctioning are NOT recommended
opts.rat: CORRECT — the 'do not' principle. Foundation for avoiding non-beneficial care.

opts.text: Escalation triggers include progressive retractions/grunting/flaring, APNEA, new/rising O2 requirement, falling SpO2 despite suctioning/positioning, poor feeding/dehydration, and lethargy — pathway HHFNC → CPAP → intubation
opts.rat: CORRECT — escalation principle. Foundation for safe upgrading of care.

opts.text: Prevention includes hand hygiene, smoke-free home/car, palivizumab for selected high-risk infants per AAP, nirsevimab for all infants entering or during their first RSV season per current immunization guidance, and maternal RSV vaccination during pregnancy
opts.rat: CORRECT — prevention principle. Foundation for future protection.

opts.text: All wheezing infants with bronchiolitis should receive routine systemic corticosteroids to suppress airway inflammation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Routine systemic corticosteroids are not recommended; they do not improve outcomes in viral bronchiolitis.

opts.text: Smoke exposure is not a meaningful factor as long as the father smokes outdoors
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Third-hand smoke and residual smoke from clothing/'smoking jackets' still expose the infant; smoke-free homes/cars/vehicles are essential.

opts.text: Maintain a patent airway and oxygenation — nasal saline + bulb suction before feeds and PRN, HOB elevation + comfort positioning, supplemental O2 for persistent SpO2 < ~ 90-92%, and minimal handling/cluster care
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Airway + oxygenation are the foundation of bronchiolitis care. RN role: airway/O2.

opts.text: Maintain hydration with small frequent feeds; NG or IV fluids if oral intake is inadequate or the work of breathing is unsafe for oral feeding
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Hydration sustains the infant and supports recovery. RN role: feeds + hydration.

opts.text: Apply contact + droplet isolation with strict hand hygiene, cohorting, and PPE; monitor for apnea, increased work of breathing, dehydration, lethargy; escalate to HHFNC → CPAP → intubation as needed
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Infection control + watchful monitoring + readiness to escalate. RN role: isolation + monitor + escalate.

opts.text: Avoid non-beneficial routine interventions — no routine albuterol, systemic steroids, antibiotics, racemic epinephrine, routine CXR, or deep suctioning
opts.rat: #4 — Prevents harm and aligns with the AAP guideline. RN role: avoid non-beneficial care.

opts.text: Teach the family (bulb suction before feeds and sleep; signs of dehydration and worsening; hand hygiene; smoke-free home/car); reinforce prevention (nirsevimab/palivizumab; maternal RSV vaccine); schedule follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Closing/ongoing. Teaching + prevention sustain the response. RN role: teach + prevention.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this bronchiolitis admission.

opts.text: Perform nasal saline drops + bulb suction before feeds, before sleep, and as needed; elevate the head of bed and use comfort positioning held by a parent; minimize handling/cluster care
opts.rat: Primary supportive interventions. RN role: airway + comfort.

opts.text: Offer small frequent oral feeds; start NG or IV fluids if intake is inadequate or the work of breathing makes oral feeding unsafe
opts.rat: Supports hydration without aspiration risk. RN role: feeds + hydration.

opts.text: Apply supplemental low-flow O2 to maintain SpO2 ≥ 90-92%; avoid over-oxygenation; wean as tolerated
opts.rat: Appropriate threshold-based oxygen use. RN role: oxygenation.

opts.text: Maintain contact + droplet isolation, cohort with other RSV+ patients, use PPE (gown, gloves, mask), and reinforce strict hand hygiene
opts.rat: Infection prevention. RN role: isolation + hand hygiene.

opts.text: Continuously monitor cardiopulmonary + SpO2 given age and reported apnea; watch for increased work of breathing, falling SpO2, poor feeding/dehydration, lethargy; escalate to HHFNC → CPAP → intubation as needed
opts.rat: Watchful monitoring + escalation readiness. RN role: monitor + escalate.

opts.text: Teach the family bulb-suction technique, signs of dehydration and worsening, strict hand hygiene, and a smoke-free home/car (residual smoke from a 'smoking jacket' still exposes the infant); reinforce prevention (nirsevimab; maternal RSV vaccine; palivizumab for high-risk per AAP)
opts.rat: Family teaching + prevention. RN role: teach + prevention.

opts.text: Give a routine course of nebulized albuterol, systemic corticosteroids, and IV ceftriaxone for the wheezing infant
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — routine bronchodilators and systemic steroids are not recommended; antibiotics are reserved for clear bacterial superinfection.

opts.text: Perform routine deep nasotracheal suctioning every 2 hours regardless of need; skip nasal saline before feeds
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — deep suctioning has no evidence and may worsen; nasal saline + bulb suction before feeds is the most evidence-based intervention.

opts.text: Obtain a routine chest X-ray and rapid viral panel on every admitted bronchiolitis patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — routine CXR and routine viral testing are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis (rapid RSV may be used selectively for isolation/cohorting and palivizumab decisions).

opts.text: Reassure the family that smoking outside is sufficient — they do not need to make the home or car smoke-free
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — residual smoke from clothing exposes the infant; smoke-free home and car are required.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Perform nasal saline + bulb suction; elevate the HOB and use comfort positioning; supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 ≥ ~ 90-92%; minimal handling/cluster care
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Airway + oxygenation are foundational. RN role: airway/O2.

opts.text: Offer small frequent feeds; start NG or IV fluids if intake is inadequate or work of breathing makes oral feeding unsafe
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Hydration sustains the infant. RN role: feeds + hydration.

opts.text: Apply contact + droplet isolation with cohorting, PPE, and strict hand hygiene; continuously monitor cardiopulmonary + SpO2 and watch for apnea/worsening; escalate to HHFNC → CPAP → intubation as needed
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Infection control + monitoring + escalation readiness. RN role: isolation + monitor + escalate.

opts.text: Avoid non-beneficial routine interventions — no routine albuterol, systemic steroids, antibiotics, racemic epinephrine, routine CXR, or deep suctioning
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Prevents harm and aligns with guideline. RN role: avoid non-beneficial care.

opts.text: Teach the family (bulb suction before feeds and sleep, signs of dehydration/worsening, hand hygiene, smoke-free home/car), reinforce prevention (nirsevimab/palivizumab/maternal RSV vaccine), schedule follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Teaching + prevention complete the response. RN role: teach + prevention.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: After nasal saline + bulb suction, HOB elevation, comfort positioning, and supplemental O2, the infant's respiratory rate falls from 62 to ~48, retractions ease, SpO2 stays ≥ 95%, and feeding improves
opts.rat: Correct supportive response — airway clearance and oxygenation work. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Oral intake stays ~ 60% of usual → NG feeds begun without aspiration; hydration is maintained without escalating respiratory support
opts.rat: Safe hydration with the right tool. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Day 2 — weaned to room air with stable SpO2, feeding ~ 75%, no further apnea, mother teaches back bulb-suction technique, signs to report, hand hygiene, and the smoke-free home plan
opts.rat: Sustained recovery + verified family understanding. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Discharge with stable SpO2 in room air, adequate feeding, family ready, and reliable follow-up; nirsevimab/palivizumab and maternal RSV vaccine discussed for prevention
opts.rat: Safe discharge with prevention reinforced. Ideal outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — routine albuterol nebs, systemic corticosteroids, and antibiotics were given; no clinical improvement and the infant developed adverse effects
opts.rat: ⚠️ Non-recommended routine interventions added burden without benefit. Demonstrates the 'do not' list.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — deep nasotracheal suctioning every 2 hours was performed; the infant became agitated, work of breathing worsened, and SpO2 fell
opts.rat: ⚠️ Deep suctioning has no evidence and may worsen status. Demonstrates the limit of nasopharyngeal suction.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the family was told smoking outside is sufficient with no smoke-free home/car counseling; the infant's recovery was prolonged and exposure continued
opts.rat: ⚠️ Residual third-hand smoke from clothing exposes the infant; smoke-free home and car are required.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the infant continued to be agitated by frequent unnecessary checks and handling; SpO2 fluctuated and feeding fell despite supportive measures
opts.rat: ⚠️ Over-handling worsens respiratory effort in bronchiolitis. Demonstrates cluster care + minimal handling.

opts.text: In the first hours after supportive care begins, before measurable improvement, vital signs and work of breathing are essentially unchanged while the infant tolerates suction, positioning, and small feeds
opts.rat: An expected stable trajectory before improvement; continue supportive care and reassess. Unchanged is appropriate here.

opts.text: Long-term — the family maintains a smoke-free home/car, hand hygiene, and the older sibling stays distanced when ill; the infant receives nirsevimab/palivizumab per protocol and the next pregnancy includes maternal RSV vaccination
opts.rat: Sustained prevention reduces future risk for the infant and household. Ideal longer-term outcome.
